query: clinical trial evidence for CEP-26401 and its effects on cognition

retrieved_docs.title: Ginkgo Biloba -- Indications -- Dementia/ Cognitive Impairment
retrieved_docs.content: In terms of treatment for existing dementia, data has been contradictory regarding the efficacy of Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb). A 52-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, multicenter study of 309 patients in 1997 concluded that EGb was safe and though modestly, it appeared to stabilize and improve the cognitive performance as well as social functioning of dementia patients for six months to 1 year. [1] Similarly, another 24-week randomized controlled trial with 410 outpatients found that treatment with EGb 761 using a once-daily dose of 240 mg was safe and demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in cognition, psychopathology, functional status and quality of life of patients and caregivers. [2] On the other hand, a randomized control trial of 513 outpatients with mild to moderate dementia of the Alzheimer type did not support the efficacy of ginkgo extract. [3] A systematic review of 36 trials in 2009 and another review of 38 trials in 2018, though demonstrated that Ginkgo biloba was relatively safe, but did not support its clinical benefit for patients with cognitive impairment and dementia. [4] [5] Conversely, a 2015 systematic review of 9 trials concluded that EGb761 at 240 mg/day was able to decelerate decline in cognition, function, behavior, and global change at 22 to 26 weeks in patients with dementia, especially for those with neuropsychiatric symptoms. [6] A 2017 study of 12 systematic reviews also suggested that at doses greater than 200mg/day for at least five months, EGb had potentially beneficial effects for patients with dementia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Major Neurocognitive Disorder (Dementia) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Aducanumab is a recombinant monoclonal antibody directed against amyloid beta that was recently approved by the FDA for the treatment of mild Alzheimer disease. Its approval remains highly controversial. The drug is costly and does not have clear, proven clinical benefits. It was approved based on positive clinical results seen in only 1 of the 2 phase III trials and aducanumab's effect on a surrogate endpoint (reducing amyloid beta plaques in the brain), which has not been proven clinically significant. [34] Lifestyle modifications to optimize cognitive function include optimizing sleep, eating an anti-inflammatory diet, adequate exercise, treating hearing or vision loss, minimizing stress, and maintaining normal blood sugar, cholesterol, and blood pressure levels. [35] Behavioral symptoms include irritability, anxiety, and depression. Antidepressants and sometimes antipsychotics can help with these symptoms. In addition, non-drug approaches like supportive care, memory training, physical exercise programs, and mental and social stimulation must be employed in symptom control. Patients and their families should be counseled about the disease and its consequences. They should be provided with all the necessary information about what to expect and how to react. Patients and their families should also be encouraged to seek social service consultations and to register with support groups and societies. Coaching caregivers on skills such as redirection and reassurance as opposed to repeated correction of patients confused due to dementia can avoid or de-escalate possible behavioral symptoms. Driving restrictions may have to be imposed.

retrieved_docs.title: Mild Cognitive Impairment -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there are no FDA-approved agents for the treatment of MCI. Several pharmacological agents have been studied for efficacy in treating MCI. These include acetylcholine esterase inhibitors donepezil, galantamine, and rivastigmine, homocysteine-lowering B vitamins, flavonoid-containing drinks, vitamin E, vitamin C, transdermal nicotine patch, piribedil, rofecoxib, tesamorelin injections, and V0191. Donepezil and other medications used for AD are not effective in reducing the progression of MCI. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacopa monnieri -- Indications -- Human Studies
retrieved_docs.content: In a study of memory acquisition, there was a randomized, double-blinded study (81 adults 55 years and older) that reported a 12-week cycle of Bacopa significantly improved memory acquisition and retention in healthy older Australians. [12] A meta-analysis incorporating results from 6 studies with 12 weeks or greater duration found that Bacopa improved memory-free recall but did not improve other aspects of cognition. These studies used a 300 to 450 mg dose of Bacopa extract standardized to 10 to 20% bacopa glycosides [13] . Bacopa has shown anxiolytic effects in people with cognitive decline. Bacopa, in combination with Gotu kola ( Centella asiatica ), has been shown in double-blind, randomized clinical trials to reduce general anxiety symptoms effectively. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Mild Cognitive Impairment -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The 2018 AAN practice guidelines advise against pharmacologic or dietary agents for MCI treatment. [15] The guidelines recommend that clinicians discuss with the patient before choosing not to offer cholinesterase inhibitors or prescribing them off-label. Patients should also be provided information about relevant clinical trials if they are interested in pharmacologic treatment. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Major Neurocognitive Disorder (Dementia) (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Aducanumab is a recombinant monoclonal antibody directed against amyloid beta that was recently approved by the FDA for treatment of mild alzheimer disease. It's approval remains highly controversial. The drug is costly and does not have clear proven clinical benefits. It was approved based on positive clinical results seen in only one of the two phase III trials, as well as aducanumab's effect on a surrogate endpoint (reducing amyloid beta plaques in the brain) which has not been proven to be clinically significant. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacopa monnieri -- Indications -- Human Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Bacopa monnieri functioning as a cognitive performance enhancer was demonstrated in a small (17 patient) randomized control trial published in 2014. They noted statistically significant improvement in cognitive performance (mental arithmetic, Stroop, letter search, visual tracking), improved mood, and a decreased cortisol response from stress after 1 dose of Bacopa (320 mg and 640 mg). A stronger effect was observed with the 640 mg dose. [8] In contrast, another small study (30 subjects) evaluated a daily dose of 450mg over a 12-week period in healthy adults, which demonstrated no significant change in cognitive testing scores (learning and memory, information processing), but a trend for decreased anxiety in the Bacopa group. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Ginkgo Biloba -- Indications -- Dementia/ Cognitive Impairment
retrieved_docs.content: In terms of preventing dementia, there is also insufficient evidence to support the use of ginkgo. The Ginkgo Evaluation of Memory (GEM) Study showed that Ginkgo biloba at 120 mg twice a day was not effective in reducing both all-cause dementia incidence and Alzheimer dementia incidence in elderly patients with normal cognition or with mild cognitive impairment. [8] Similarly, the GuidAge clinical trial conducted in patients aged 70 years or older who spontaneously reported memory complaints to their primary care physician in France. This trial randomized patients with either 120 mg standardized Ginkgo biloba extract or matching placebo and did not support the benefit of long-term use of standardized EGb in reducing the risk of progression to Alzheimer disease throughout five years. [9] A meta-analysis of two trials involving 5889 participants showed no significant difference in the rate of developing dementia between Ginkgo biloba and the placebo in late-life. [10] A 2012 meta-analysis did not find support for the use of Ginkgo biloba in enhancing cognitive function in healthy adults. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indication
retrieved_docs.content: Aducanumab is approved to treat Alzheimer disease in patients who have MCI-AD or who are in the mild dementia stage of the disease. [11] [12] [11] The first iteration of the package insert described the indication simply as "Alzheimer disease," but the indication was given greater specificity within days of the accelerated approval. [7] Aducanumab has not been studied to date in individuals without cognitive impairment or in those who have progressed beyond the mild stage of the disease. Aducanumab's continued use and full approval will require further verification of its clinical benefits in post-approval studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Clinical Significance -- Neuropsychiatric adverse effects
retrieved_docs.content: Cognitive function assessment: Conduct systematic evaluation of cognitive function, including attention, memory, word-finding ability, and processing speed, at baseline and every 3 months. When clinically indicated, use standardized tools, eg, the Montreal Cognitive Assessment or Mini-Mental State Examination. Monitor for reports of difficulty concentrating, confusion, or "brain fog."

retrieved_docs.title: Mild Cognitive Impairment -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Non-pharmacologic interventions such as exercise and cognitive training have also been studied for MCI treatment. A six-month randomized controlled trial resistance training exercise twice weekly showed improved cognitive function compared to weekly balance and tone exercises. [2] Another study showed that multicomponent exercise could improve cognitive function in MCI. A systematic review and meta-analysis have shown the usefulness of cognitive interventions on metacognition and activities of daily living. [1] Cognitively stimulating activities, such as crossword puzzles, card games, and frequent socialization, can help prevent the progression of MCI. [6] The AAN guidelines recommend regular exercise (twice per week) and cognitive interventions as part of management for general health benefits and low risk. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Disease onset is insidious, causing a gradual decline of behavioral and cognitive abilities that manifest as an initial short-term memory impairment accompanied eventually by impaired language, attention, comprehension, and executive functioning. [1] Current symptomatic management of Alzheimer disease entails treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors and N -methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) antagonists, as there is no cure or curative treatment to date. [1] [6] The presence of 1 or 2 APOE ε4 alleles increases the risk for AD development.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacopa monnieri -- Indications -- Human Studies
retrieved_docs.content: A meta-analysis incorporating 9 randomized controlled trials (437 subjects) demonstrated an improved speed of attention and cognition and decreased reaction time. [10] In a randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled study, patients (54 adults) were given a 300 mg standardized extract of either Bacopa or placebo. Measurements were obtained by evaluating tasks of attention, memory, and psychological state at baseline and following a 12-week trial. The treated group demonstrated enhanced delayed word recall memory scores and increased ability to ignore irrelevant information (Stroop test) relative to placebo. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: New and specific disease-modifying therapies are being developed, and the FDA has recently approved some. For example, Aducanemab was approved by the FDA in June 2020. The FDA recently approved Lecanemab. Donanemab is also expected to receive FDA approval soon. These monoclonal antibodies act by removing amyloid from the brain as immunotherapy. Remternetug is another one of these promising amyloid-targeting immunotherapy drugs in the pipeline. [47] Aducanemab received accelerated FDA approval in June 2020. It was shown to reduce amyloid-beta plaque in the brain. However, it missed the primary phase III trial endpoint of clinical improvement. [48] Lecanemab received accelerated FDA approval in January 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain. The phase III trial showed a 27% slowing of disease progression. [49] Donanemab is expected to receive FDA approval in 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain and slowed cognitive decline by 35%. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Deficits -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of cognitive deficits depends on what actually is causing impairment. If it is caused by an illness or a condition, then it is likely to recover after the treatment. Infections and metabolic syndromes, depression, thyroid disorders, and medication effects are some curable causes of cognitive decline. For cognitive disorders, a detailed assessment and management are required, and the interventions focus mainly on the improvement of quality of life and the limitation of residual defects.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The package insert for aducanumab defines no contraindications for its use in patients with MCI-AD or in patients with mild-stage AD.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: The physiological effects of drugs encompass a range of pharmacologic actions, and one specific category within this broader group is central nervous system stimulants. Central nervous system stimulants are agents that produce stimulating effects on the brain and spinal cord, influencing alertness, mood, cognition, and energy levels. Recognizing that central nervous system stimulants are a child concept of the overall physiological effects of drugs helps clinicians understand their mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential adverse effects, thereby supporting more informed prescribing, monitoring, and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1678
retrieved_docs.content: Bamifylline, etofylline, and propentofylline are all classified as Xanthine derivatives that function as psychostimulants and are employed as agents for attention‑deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and as nootropic substances. Each of these compounds belongs to the broader group of Xanthine‑based psychostimulant agents, sharing similar mechanisms of action that enhance central nervous system activity and cognitive performance. Recognizing that bamifylline, etofylline, and propentofylline are members of this pharmacologic class helps clinicians understand their potential therapeutic roles, side‑effect profiles, and the rationale for using them in the management of ADHD and cognitive enhancement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” encompasses a range of agents that are classified together because of their stimulant and cognition‑enhancing properties. Included in this group are acylcarnitine, cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, fipexide, nizofenone, pipradrol, and prolintane. This category is itself a subclass of psychostimulants and agents used for ADHD and nootropics, reflecting its role in therapies aimed at improving attention, alertness, and cognitive function. By grouping these diverse compounds under a single ATC class, clinicians and researchers can more readily identify and compare medications that share similar mechanisms of central nervous system stimulation and nootropic effects, facilitating informed prescribing and pharmacological study.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2123
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” encompasses a diverse group of agents that share stimulant or cognition‑enhancing properties. Included in this class are acylcarnitine, cytidine diphosphate choline, fipexide, oxiracetam, pipradrol, and prolintane. By grouping these compounds together, the classification reflects their common pharmacological actions, which are relevant for therapeutic strategies aimed at improving alertness, cognitive function, or neuroprotection in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: hub that connects dementia, post‑concussive states, depressive mood, psychotic features, and cholinergic antagonism within a unified nosological framework, supporting comprehensive assessment and targeted management of patients whose psychiatric symptoms arise from or are intertwined with underlying medical or neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a subclass of psychoanaleptics, meaning they belong to the broader group of central‑nervous‑system stimulants that enhance mental activity. This classification reflects the pharmacologic action of anti‑dementia agents, which aim to improve cognition and counteract the decline associated with dementia by stimulating neural pathways, a property shared with other psychoanaleptic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Oxanamide, mephenoxalone, and chlormezanon are all classified as sedative agents. Each of these drugs functions to depress central nervous system activity, producing calming, anxiolytic, or sleep‑inducing effects. In the hierarchy of pharmacologic categories, sedatives are a subset of neurological agents, meaning they belong to the broader group of medications that act on the nervous system to modify its function. Recognizing that Oxanamide, mephenoxalone, and chlormezanon share this common classification helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential therapeutic uses, and related safety considerations within the context of neurological drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: as تبلد المشاعر (blunted affect). Both متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية and حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة belong to متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ), linking organic hallucination syndrome and mild confusional states to the broader cholinergic antagonist syndrome. Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere are positioned as children of mood disorders, anxiety disorders, and miscellaneous mental health disorders, reflecting their recognition across multiple diagnostic domains. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how drug‑induced and medically‑related anxiety and mood disorders are integrated into broader categories of organic and miscellaneous mental health conditions, and how senility and organic mental disorders function as a central classification hub that connects dementia, post‑concussive states, depressive mood, psychotic features, and cholinergic antagonism within a unified nosological framework, supporting comprehensive assessment and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 979
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as cholinesterasové inhibitory, reflecting their broader relationship to cholinesterase‑inhibiting agents. These drugs exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, thereby increasing acetylcholine availability in the central nervous system. Donepezil is a specific member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug class, exemplifying the use of acetylcholinesterase inhibition in the treatment of dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2632
retrieved_docs.content: Xanthine derivatives, which function as psychostimulants and are employed as agents for attention‑deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and as nootropics, include the drugs bamifylline and etofylline. Both bamifylline and etofylline belong to this class of xanthine‑based compounds, sharing the pharmacological profile of stimulating central nervous system activity and being used to enhance cognitive performance and manage ADHD symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 106
retrieved_docs.content: ADHD therapy. Within this group, deanol formulations contain hydrocortisone 10 MG/ML topical cream as an inactive ingredient, a detail relevant for patients with corticosteroid sensitivity. Centrally acting sympathomimetics, a subgroup of psychostimulants, include atomoxetine, methamfetamin, methylfenidát, and modafinil, illustrating the range of stimulatory agents that act on central nervous system pathways to improve alertness and cognition. Psychoanaleptics also encompass anti‑dementia drugs as a subclass; memantin is listed among these anti‑dementia agents in the ATC classification, linking agents that mitigate cognitive decline with those that stimulate mental function. Overall, the knowledge base highlights the overlapping pharmacologic profiles of antidepressants, psychostimulants, nootropics, and anti‑dementia drugs, the hierarchical organization of these classes, and critical drug‑drug and drug‑condition interactions that inform clinical decision‑making and formulation

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2220
retrieved_docs.content: Aniracetam is classified as a member of the broader group of nootropic substances, indicating that it belongs to the class of compounds known for cognitive‑enhancing effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 636
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting sympathomimetics is a pharmacologic class that includes the agents atomoxetine, methamfetamin, methylfenidát, and modafinil. These drugs share a mechanism of action that stimulates central sympathetic pathways, increasing neurotransmitter activity such as norepinephrine and dopamine, which underlies their therapeutic effects and side‑effect profiles. Atomoxetine is primarily prescribed for attention‑deficit hyperactivity disorder, while methamfetamin, methylfenidát, and modafinil are used for conditions such as narcolepsy, ADHD, and, in some cases, off‑label cognitive enhancement. The presence of methamfetamin can be characterized by specific detection parameters—denoted by the classification “药物类 已鉴定的:存在与否或特征标识:时间点:体液:名义型”—which describe whether the drug is detectable, the timing of detection, the type of body fluid sampled, and its nominal classification. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same centrally acting sympathomimetic class helps clinicians anticipate similar adverse effects, assess abuse potential, and interpret drug‑testing results in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” encompasses a diverse group of agents that share central nervous system‑stimulating or cognition‑enhancing properties. Included in this class are the racetam derivative aniracetam, the metabolic compound acylkarnitin, the choline precursor cytidindifosfátcholin, the experimental cognitive enhancer fipexide, and the stimulants nizofenone, pipradrol, and prolintane. Aniracetam is specifically identified as a member of the broader group of nootropic substances, underscoring its role as a cognitive‑enhancing drug. Grouping these compounds together reflects their common therapeutic focus on improving alertness, attention, or memory, which is important for clinicians when selecting agents for conditions that benefit from psychostimulant or nootropic effects.
